Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Satomi Yoshitaka|Satomi Yoshitaka]], and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Kururi Station|Kururi Station]] on the [[:en:Kururi Line|Kururi Line]] (JR East)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q22118623]] Wikidata name: Kururi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kururi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kururi Jinja.JPG | location = [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]] [[:en:Kimitsu|Kimitsu City]] Urata 14-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude minutes = 17 | Latitude seconds = 21 | Longitude = 140 | Longitude minutes = 4 | Longitude seconds = 58 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = [[:en:Jian (era)|Chian era]] 1 (1021) | festival = [[:en:July 22|July 22]] | location = {{ill|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.289167|N|140.082778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|WD=Q1105844}} }}
'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Urata, [[:en:Kimitsu|Kimitsu City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in [[:en:Jian (era)|Chian era]] 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]], after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as [[:en:Shogun|Shōgun]], he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Satomi Yoshitaka|Satomi Yoshitaka]], and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Kururi Station|Kururi Station]] on the [[:en:Kururi Line|Kururi Line]] (JR East)

== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』

'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Urata, [[:en:Kimitsu|Kimitsu City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in [[:en:Jian (era)|Chian era]] 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]], after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as [[:en:Shogun|Shōgun]], he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Satomi Yoshitaka|Satomi Yoshitaka]], and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Kururi Station|Kururi Station]] on the [[:en:Kururi Line|Kururi Line]] (JR East)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』
== See Also == * [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kiminavi.jp/detail/index_35.html Kururi Shrine Main Hall] (Kimitsu City Official Tourism Information)

In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from [[:en:Kururi Station|Kururi Station]] on the [[:en:Kururi Line|Kururi Line]] (JR East)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』
== See Also == * [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]]
== External Links == * [http://www.kiminavi.jp/detail/index_35.html Kururi Shrine Main Hall] (Kimitsu City Official Tourism Information)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q22118623]] Wikidata name: Kururi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kururi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kururi Jinja.JPG | location = {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}} {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}} Urata 14-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude minutes = 17 | Latitude seconds = 21 | Longitude = 140 | Longitude minutes = 4 | Longitude seconds = 58 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) | festival = {{ill|July 22|en|July 22|WD=Q2725|lt=July 22}} | location = {{ill|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.289167|N|140.082778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|WD=Q1105844}} }}
'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Urata, {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shōgun}}, he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|WD=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line}} (JR East)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』
== See Also == * {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}
== External Links == * [http://www.kiminavi.jp/detail/index_35.html Kururi Shrine Main Hall] (Kimitsu City Official Tourism Information)
==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kururi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kururi Jinja.JPG | location = {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}} {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}} Urata 14-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude minutes = 17 | Latitude seconds = 21 | Longitude = 140 | Longitude minutes = 4 | Longitude seconds = 58 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) | festival = {{ill|July 22|en|July 22|WD=Q2725|lt=July 22}} | location = {{ill|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.289167|N|140.082778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|WD=Q1105844}} }}
'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Urata, {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shōgun}}, he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|WD=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line}} (JR East)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』
== See Also == * {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}
== External Links == * [http://www.kiminavi.jp/detail/index_35.html Kururi Shrine Main Hall] (Kimitsu City Official Tourism Information)

It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shōgun}}, he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|WD=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line}} (JR East)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』
== See Also == * {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}
== External Links == * [http://www.kiminavi.jp/detail/index_35.html Kururi Shrine Main Hall] (Kimitsu City Official Tourism Information)

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kururi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kururi Jinja.JPG | location = {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}} {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}} Urata 14-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude minutes = 17 | Latitude seconds = 21 | Longitude = 140 | Longitude minutes = 4 | Longitude seconds = 58 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) | festival = {{ill|July 22|en|July 22|WD=Q2725|lt=July 22}} | location = {{ill|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.289167|N|140.082778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|WD=Q1105844}} }}
'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Urata, {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shōgun}}, he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|WD=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line}} (JR East)

Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|WD=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line}} (JR East)

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kururi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kururi Jinja.JPG | location = {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}} {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}} Urata 14-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude minutes = 17 | Latitude seconds = 21 | Longitude = 140 | Longitude minutes = 4 | Longitude seconds = 58 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}; {{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) | festival = {{ill|July 22|en|July 22|WD=Q2725|lt=July 22}} | location = {{ill|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.289167|N|140.082778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|WD=Q1105844}} }}
'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Urata, {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}}. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era}} 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shōgun}}, he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|WD=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line}} (JR East)

Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station|1=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line|1=Kururi Line}} (JR East)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』
== See Also == * {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain|1=Kururi Domain}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久留里神社]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kururi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kururi Jinja.JPG | location = {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City|1=Kimitsu}} Urata 14-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude minutes = 17 | Latitude seconds = 21 | Longitude = 140 | Longitude minutes = 4 | Longitude seconds = 58 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}; {{ill|Izanami|WD=Q682306|1=Izanami}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} | Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era|1=Jian (era)}} 1 (1021) | festival = {{ill|July 22|en|July 22|WD=Q2725|lt=July 22|1=July 22}} | location = {{ill|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|1=Kimitsu}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.289167|N|140.082778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|WD=Q1105844|1=Taira no Tadatsune}} }}
'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Urata, {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|WD=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City|1=Kimitsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|WD=Q1150852|lt=Chian era|1=Jian (era)}} 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shōgun|1=Shogun}}, he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain|1=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|WD=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka|1=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|WD=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station|1=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|WD=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line|1=Kururi Line}} (JR East)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kimitsu City History Compilation Committee, 『君津市史』 * Cultural Affairs Division, Chiba Prefecture, 『千葉県史料 近世篇 久留里藩制一班』 * Kururi Castle Reconstruction Support Association, 『久留里城誌』

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kururi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kururi Jinja.JPG | location = {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|qid=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City|1=Kimitsu}} Urata 14-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude minutes = 17 | Latitude seconds = 21 | Longitude = 140 | Longitude minutes = 4 | Longitude seconds = 58 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|qid=Q2842779|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}; {{ill|Izanami|qid=Q682306|1=Izanami}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Ōjin|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} | Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} | consecration_year = {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|qid=Q1150852|lt=Chian era|1=Jian (era)}} 1 (1021) | festival = {{ill|July 22|en|July 22|qid=Q2725|lt=July 22|1=July 22}} | location = {{ill|Kimitsu|qid=Q637928|1=Kimitsu}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.289167|N|140.082778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|qid=Q1105844|1=Taira no Tadatsune}} }}
'''Kururi Shrine''' (久留里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Urata, {{ill|Kimitsu|en|Kimitsu|qid=Q637928|lt=Kimitsu City|1=Kimitsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. It was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qid=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, and others.
== History == Kururi Shrine was established in {{ill|Jian (era)|en|Jian (era)|qid=Q1150852|lt=Chian era|1=Jian (era)}} 1 (1021) by Taira no Tadatsune, the lord of Kururi Castle, as Urata Myōkendō. It was also known as Hosoda Myōken. There is a legend that {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined the deity and called it "Hosoda Myōken," claiming that he married a celestial maiden who bore him a child. On July 22, 1192, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|qid=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}, after landing at Katsuyama from Manazuru, prayed for victory at this shrine. Following his unification of Japan and appointment as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shōgun|1=Shogun}}, he instructed his disciple, Monk Raitada, to hold a festival of gratitude on this date, which became the shrine's major festival day.
Initially located adjacent to its current site, the shrine was referred to as Hosoda. A monument commemorating its history, erected by the Mori family, who served as chief retainers of the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|qid=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain|1=Kururi Domain}}, stands near the shrine.<ref>『君津市史 金石文編』469p</ref>
Devoutly worshiped by warlords including {{ill|Takeda Masakatsu|ja|武田真勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Masakatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Satomi Yoshitaka|en|Satomi Yoshitaka|qid=Q5366204|lt=Satomi Yoshitaka|1=Satomi Yoshitaka}}, and feudal rulers such as the Ōsuka, Tsuchiya, and Sakai clans during the Edo period, the shrine also received patronage from Kururi Domain lords. In 1717, {{ill|Kuroda Naozumi|ja|黒田直純|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Naozumi|1=Kuroda Naozumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10518200}} dedicated a vow for the construction of Kururi Castle, and subsequent domain lords continued this reverence. In the early {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1912年|1=1912|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2077}}, various deities, including {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q461258}}, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島比売命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, and {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之開耶比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were enshrined here.
== Additional Notes == * Kururi is celebrated for its water quality, being selected by the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|qid=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)}} as one of the "Heisei Famous Water Hundred." Sacred water springs within the shrine grounds attract visitors seeking blessings at this power spot. * The shrine features many turtle motifs, reflecting the belief that {{ill|Myōken|ja|妙見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見菩薩|1=Myōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10960623}}, a deified representation of the North Star, rides a turtle. * According to legend, Masakado’s third son, Yoritane, received a divine message while visiting the shrine, leading to the construction of a castle in Urata and the naming of the Kururi area.
== festival == The annual festival includes rituals held on July 22. However, in recent years, the portable shrine procession has been conducted on the nearest Saturday, celebrated as the "Kururi Summer Festival."
== Cultural Properties == [[File:Kururijinja.jpg|thumb|Kururi Shrine Worship Hall]] <small>(Designation type and date in parentheses)</small> * **Kururi Shrine Main Hall** (Designated as a Municipal Cultural Property, Building, on August 4, 1975): The hall retains remnants of the shrine’s historical syncretism with Buddhism. The worship hall collapsed during Typhoon No. 15 in 2019.
== Access == * A 20-minute walk from {{ill|Kururi Station|en|Kururi Station|qid=Q6447492|lt=Kururi Station|1=Kururi Station}} on the {{ill|Kururi Line|en|Kururi Line|qid=Q498157|lt=Kururi Line|1=Kururi Line}} (JR East)
